findings.description: The worked example assigns post(C)=7, but in the described DFS traversal starting at A that visits B before C, DFS would explore A→B→D (D finishes first, post(D)=2 or similar), then backtrack to B (post(B)), then backtrack to A and visit C→D (D already visited), so C would finish before B, not after; assigning post(C)=7 and post(B)=5 is inconsistent with visiting B before C in a standard DFS on A→B→D, A→C→D, because B's subtree (including D) must finish before C is even started, making post(B) > post(C) impossible when B is visited first.
findings.suggested_fix: If DFS visits B before C, then B's subtree finishes before C is explored, so post(B) > post(C); swap the assignments to post(B)=7 and post(C)=5 (or renumber consistently) so the example is self-consistent.

findings.description: The paragraph concludes the topological order is A, C, B, D from the post numbers post(A)=8, post(C)=7, post(B)=5, post(D)=4, but if DFS visits B before C (as stated), the correct decreasing-post-number order consistent with that traversal would be A, B, C, D (with post(B) > post(C)), not A, C, B, D.
findings.suggested_fix: Correct the post-number assignments so they are consistent with visiting B before C, yielding the order A, B, C, D, or change the narrative to say DFS visits C before B to justify post(C)=7 > post(B)=5.
